structured_choices.data_source: High-resolution retinal fundus images from a multi-center cohort spanning 5 years. Images sourced from at least 3 geographically and demographically distinct clinical centers to ensure diversity in patient populations, imaging equipment, and lighting conditions. Minimum 5,000 images across all severity grades; target distribution should reflect population prevalence (approximately 65% no retinopathy, 20% mild, 10% moderate, 5% severe) to match deployment context.
structured_choices.label_definition: Severity labels assigned by consensus grading from a panel of at least 2 independent certified ophthalmologists using the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) severity scale or equivalent validated standard. Consensus is defined as agreement between ≥2/3 raters; images with disagreement are adjudicated by a senior ophthalmologist. The resulting label is treated as ground truth. For the primary comparison, the 'traditional grading system' baseline is defined as the initial consensus severity assignment used operationally at each center.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified random split by patient ID (not image) to prevent data leakage from the same patient appearing in train/validation/test. Stratification occurs on both severity grade and clinical center to preserve class distribution and demographic diversity across splits. Split proportions: 70% train (n≈3,500 images), 15% validation (n≈750 images), 15% test (n≈750 images). Patient-level stratification ensures that all images from a single patient appear in exactly one fold.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All image preprocessing transformations (normalization, standardization, augmentation parameters) are fit on the training set only. Fit statistics (e.g., per-channel mean and standard deviation) are computed from training images, then applied identically to validation and test sets. Data augmentation (rotation, mild brightness adjustment, elastic deformation) is applied only during training; validation and test images are preprocessed without augmentation to reflect deployment conditions.
structured_choices.features: Raw pixel intensities from high-resolution fundus images (typically 2048×2048 or equivalent) are used directly as input to the CNN. No hand-crafted features are engineered. The CNN learns hierarchical representations. Pre-processing steps applied uniformly: (1) Resize to 512×512 for computational efficiency while retaining clinically relevant detail; (2) Normalize pixel values to [0, 1]; (3) Per-channel standardization using training set statistics; (4) Training augmentation includes: rotation (±15°), horizontal/vertical flips, mild brightness/contrast adjustment (±10%), and elastic deformations to improve robustness to real-world imaging variation.
structured_choices.model: A standard deep convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture appropriate for medical image classification. Candidates include: EfficientNet-B3 or ResNet-50 pre-trained on ImageNet, fine-tuned on the retinal fundus domain. The architecture includes a global average pooling layer followed by a fully connected layer with softmax output for 4-class classification (no retinopathy, mild, moderate, severe). Weights are initialized from ImageNet pre-training to leverage learned visual features, then adapted to the ophthalmology task.
structured_choices.baseline: The baseline is the initial consensus expert grading system used operationally at each center. To ensure fair comparison: (1) Baseline predictions are the consensus severity labels assigned by the panel of ophthalmologists during the labeling phase; (2) Baseline is evaluated on the same stratified test set as the CNN; (3) The baseline receives no additional tuning—it represents the current clinical standard; (4) Baseline performance is reported using the same metrics (AUROC, F1-score, confusion matrix) as the CNN. This is the correct baseline because it represents the operational comparator: the question is whether the CNN outperforms human expert consensus, which is the decision-maker the model would replace or augment in practice.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC) using the one-vs-rest approach for multi-class classification. For each severity grade, compute AUROC treating that grade as positive and all others as negative; report the macro-averaged AUROC (mean across all 4 classes). Justification: AUROC is threshold-independent and appropriate for the operational goal of ranking patients by retinopathy risk to prioritize referrals. It accounts for the cost asymmetry in the problem: false negatives (missing retinopathy) are costlier than false positives (unnecessary referrals), and AUROC captures model discrimination ability across all decision thresholds. AUROC is robust to class imbalance and does not require a pre-specified decision threshold.
secondary_metrics: Per-class F1-score (harmonic mean of precision and recall) to assess balanced performance on minority classes (moderate, severe) where clinical error is most consequential
Weighted F1-score (weighted by class support in the test set) to assess overall accuracy while accounting for class imbalance
Confusion matrix and per-class sensitivity (recall) and specificity to identify whether the model systematically misclassifies particular severity grades
Calibration plot (predicted probability vs. observed frequency) to assess whether the model's confidence estimates are reliable for downstream decision-making
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, dropout, L2 regularization, batch size, number of epochs) is performed using the validation set (15% of data). Grid search or random search over a pre-defined hyperparameter space is conducted, with early stopping based on validation AUROC. The validation set is used only for model selection; it is not used to evaluate the final model. The test set is held out and used exactly once, at the end of the experiment, to report final performance. No information from the test set informs any hyperparameter choice.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is partitioned into two sub-folds to enable one final internal consistency check: (a) a 'held-out test set' (8/10 of the 15%, n≈600 images) used once at the very end to report final metrics; (b) a 'final internal validation set' (2/10 of the 15%, n≈150 images) set aside from the beginning, held out from all tuning, used only if a material discrepancy is detected between validation and test performance as a diagnostic. This second set guards against overfitting to the validation set but is not used for model selection. Standard practice: the primary test set is touched only once at the end and reported in a single table of results.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Subjectivity in expert grading is addressed by requiring consensus (≥2/3 agreement) and adjudication of disagreements by a senior ophthalmologist. Inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa) is computed and reported to quantify label noise. To control for imaging variation across centers and equipment, stratified splitting by center ensures each fold represents all centers proportionally. Sensitivity analysis is performed: models are trained separately on each center's data and tested on held-out data from the same center, as well as on held-out data from other centers (transfer learning check). If performance degrades significantly under cross-center transfer, this is reported and scoped as a limitation. Patient demographics (age, ethnicity, presence of comorbidities) are tracked and reported by fold to verify that stratification achieved balance. No claim is made about generalization to populations or imaging equipment not represented in the dataset. The experiment establishes whether the CNN outperforms consensus expert grading on the specific multi-center cohort; it does not establish superiority in all real-world contexts without additional prospective validation.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN for Diabetic Retinopathy Severity Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling
This experiment uses a multi-center cohort of high-resolution retinal fundus images collected over five years from at least three geographically and demographically distinct clinical centers. The dataset comprises approximately 5,000 images, distributed across four severity grades according to the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) scale: no retinopathy (~3,250 images), mild (~1,000), moderate (~500), and severe (~250). This distribution reflects population prevalence and ensures the model trains on realistic class proportions.

Labels are assigned through a consensus grading process: two independent certified ophthalmologists grade each image independently using the ETDRS standard. Consensus is defined as agreement between ≥2 of 3 raters; images with initial disagreement are adjudicated by a senior ophthalmologist. This multi-rater approach mitigates subjective grading variability, which is a known confounder. Inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa) is computed and reported alongside results.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy
Data is split at the patient level to prevent leakage: all images from a single patient appear in exactly one fold. Stratification occurs on both severity grade and clinical center, ensuring each fold preserves class distribution and center diversity. The split allocation is: 70% training (n≈3,500 images), 15% validation (n≈750 images), 15% test (n≈750 images). Stratification-by-center guards against the confounder of equipment and demographic variation; sensitivity analysis later evaluates whether the model generalizes across centers.

### 3. Image Preprocessing and Feature Engineering
All image preprocessing transformations are fit on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. Preprocessing steps are: (1) Resize to 512×512 to maintain clinical detail while ensuring computational efficiency; (2) Normalize pixel values to [0, 1]; (3) Per-channel standardization using training set mean and standard deviation. Training augmentation includes mild rotation (±15°), flips, brightness/contrast adjustment (±10%), and elastic deformation to improve robustness. Validation and test images undergo no augmentation, reflecting deployment conditions. No hand-crafted features are engineered; the CNN learns end-to-end representations from raw pixel intensities.

### 4. Model and Baseline
The model is a standard deep CNN (e.g., EfficientNet-B3 or ResNet-50) pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned on the retinal fundus dataset. The architecture includes global average pooling and a softmax layer for 4-class classification.

The baseline is the consensus expert grading system currently used operationally. To ensure fair comparison, the baseline is evaluated on the same stratified test set using identical metrics. The baseline represents the decision-maker the model would replace or augment in clinical practice; this is the correct counterfactual.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
The primary metric is macro-averaged AUROC across all four severity grades (one-vs-rest approach). AUROC is justified because: (1) it is threshold-independent, enabling flexible operational deployment; (2) it accounts for the asymmetric cost of errors (missing retinopathy is costlier than unnecessary referrals); (3) it is robust to class imbalance. Secondary metrics include per-class F1-score (to identify if the model fails on minority classes), weighted F1-score, sensitivity/specificity by grade (for clinical interpretability), and calibration plots (to assess reliability of predicted probabilities).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation
Hyperparameter tuning is performed using the validation set, with early stopping based on validation AUROC. The test set is held out and used only once, at the very end, to report final performance. No test set information informs any tuning decision.

### 7. Controls and Sensitivity Analysis
Inter-rater reliability is computed to quantify label noise. Stratification by center ensures demographic and equipment diversity across folds. A sensitivity analysis trains separate models for each center and tests both within-center and cross-center, to assess generalization. Patient demographics are tracked and reported by fold to verify balance. The experiment establishes whether the CNN outperforms consensus expert grading on this specific multi-center cohort; claims are scoped to exclude populations or equipment not represented in the data.
